front: Building: modernist house in Poissy raised on pilotis, exemplifying the Five Points of Architecture
Building: concrete "machine for living" constructed outside Paris
Building: ground floor curved to accommodate the turning radius of a 1927 Citroen; appears to float on five pilotis
Building: a darker "Southern Hemisphere shadow" version of this house in Canberra was designed by Ashton Raggatt MacDougall
Location: planned city in India designed with cousin Pierre Jeanneret, based on the Ville Radieuse concept
Location: Capitol Complex features a truncated hyperboloid atop its concrete Palace of Assembly
Location: city with an 85-foot-tall multi-colored sheet metal Open Hand Monument that rotates in the wind, intended as "a sign of peace and reconciliation"
Location: city where this architect designed a three-sided cubic Tower of Shadows with perforated walls to demonstrate sun control
Location: city whose High Court building features brise soleils
Location: Maxwell Fry and Jane Drew worked alongside this architect and his cousin on this planned city
Architect: wrote a treatise containing the Five Points of Architecture: pilotis, roof gardens, free plan, free facade, ribbon windows
work: Towards a New Architecture (Vers une architecture, 1923)

front: Architect: declared "a house is a machine for living in"
work: Towards a New Architecture (Vers une architecture, 1923)

front: Architect: wrote a treatise with a chapter titled "Eyes that do not See"
work: Towards a New Architecture (Vers une architecture, 1923)

front: Architect: called the Parthenon a "machine for stirring emotion" and an example of "selection applied to an established standard"
work: Towards a New Architecture (Vers une architecture, 1923)

front: Architect: authorship of this treatise disputed by Amedee Ozenfant, who claimed credit since its essays were published under a shared pseudonym in L'Esprit Nouveau
work: Towards a New Architecture (Vers une architecture, 1923)

front: Architect: devised a proportional system called the Modulor, based on the height of a six-foot-tall man with a raised hand, incorporating the golden ratio
Architect: designed a series of chairs given his initials and a number, following the Modulor system; one appeared on a Spy x Family volume cover
Architect: designed an unrealized ideal city with identical housing blocks and cross-shaped skyscrapers connected by greenery on a Cartesian grid
Architect: elements of his ideal city plan were used by Lucio Costa and Oscar Niemeyer in designing Brasilia
Architect: his Plan Voisin, named after an automobile maker, would have gutted central Paris to install 18 cruciform towers
Architect: co-founded Purism with Amedee Ozenfant, a less-decorative offshoot of Cubism seeking to depict objects in simplified form
Architect: co-wrote the Purist manifesto Apres le Cubisme with Ozenfant

back: Notre Dame du Haut, Ronchamp (Le Corbusier)

front: Building: chapel at Ronchamp with a curved roof resembling an airplane wing that lets in a crack of light
Building: 1958 World Expo pavilion with nine hyperbolic paraboloids, designed with Iannis Xenakis; featured composition Concret PH
Building: concrete housing block incorporating terrace gardens and pilotis; inspired the VM Houses in Copenhagen
Building: Dominican convent near Lyon, designed in collaboration with Iannis Xenakis
back: Convent of Sainte Marie de La Tourette (Le Corbusier)
work: Convent of Sainte Marie de La Tourette

front: Architect: Swiss-French architect born Charles-Edouard Jeanneret
Architect: his design for the League of Nations was rejected for not being presented in Indian ink
Architect: co-designed the UN Headquarters with Oscar Niemeyer
Architect: his easternmost building is a National Museum of Western Art in Ueno Park, Tokyo
Architect: won three contests to design Moscow's Tsentrosoyuz building
Architect: developed an unused plan for Algiers in which homes would be built under a massive highway
Architect: oversaw the Ministry of Education and Health in Rio de Janeiro; student Lucio Costa designed Brasilia
Architect: designed the Mill Owners' Building and Sarabhai House alongside mentee Balkrishna Doshi
Architect: once called Piranesi "horrible, ugly, and idiotic"
Architect: befriended by Minnette de Silva, the youngest and first Asian delegate to a CIAM meeting
Architect: reportedly carried his wife's vertebrae around after she died
Architect: urban planning theories criticized by Jane Jacobs
Architect: the Athens Charter was based on his syndicalist-inspired Radiant City concept
Architect: helped popularize the use of brise soleils (sun-breaking devices)
Architect: Villa Jeanneret-Perret is conjoined to the Villa La Roche and now hosts a foundation
